text:
I'd like to present to you the inaugural celebration of Backwards Day, observed annually on March 15th, which has become the most confusing yet oddly unifying holiday in the modern calendar.

On Backwards Day, the entire world attempts to do everything in reverse. Breakfast is served at dinner time, consisting of coffee and toast that must be eaten from the plate towards your mouth rather than the conventional method. People greet each other by saying goodbye first, then hello, creating conversations that start with "See you never!" and end with "Nice to meet you!" Handshakes are performed with the left hand exclusively, and everyone walks backwards to work, which has resulted in a staggering increase in property damage and creative parking lot design.

Businesses close their cash registers at the start of the day and open them at closing time, meaning customers pay when they leave rather than when they arrive. This has created an unexpected honor system that has somehow increased retail integrity by approximately twelve percent. Restaurants serve dessert first, followed by salads, then the main course, followed by the appetizer, which has led to some profound existential conversations about why soup was ever considered a starting point for anything.

The holiday's most cherished tradition involves everyone reversing their daily commute, so people who normally drive north drive south instead, creating what traffic experts describe as "organized chaos that somehow works better than normal Tuesday." Social media is celebrated by posting only in reply to old comments from 2015, creating a surreal time-travel experience where people apologize for things they haven't done yet.

The evening culminates in goodbye parties, where attendees arrive as other people are leaving, creating a strange temporal paradox of celebration. Cake is cut into the shape of the cake before it was cut. Everyone leaves feeling they've had a wonderful time experiencing something they can't quite remember.
